name: treesitter-patterns
description: Universal patterns for tree-sitter code parsing. Covers AST visitors, query patterns, and language plugin development. Framework-agnostic.
capabilities: Parse source code into AST using tree-sitter parsers
Navigate and traverse AST nodes with visitors
Write tree-sitter query patterns for code extraction
Handle parse errors and edge cases gracefully
Build language plugins with framework-agnostic patterns
Extract functions, classes, and imports from code
author: Consiliency
securitySummary: This skill contains only markdown documentation with tree-sitter code examples. No executable code, network calls, file operations, or cryptographic operations exist. Static findings are false positives from documentation patterns being misidentified.
valueStatement: Working with tree-sitter requires understanding AST navigation, query patterns, and visitor implementations. This skill provides ready-to-use patterns for parsing any programming language.
limitations: Does not install tree-sitter language parsers automatically
Does not execute or evaluate parsed code
Does not handle parser compilation or grammar creation

useCases.title: Extract code structures
useCases.description: Pull function definitions, class declarations, and imports from any codebase for analysis.

useCases.title: Build language tools
useCases.description: Create new language plugins using visitor patterns and query optimizations.

useCases.title: Generate code references
useCases.description: Parse source files to auto-generate API documentation and code inventories.

promptTemplates.title: Parse function
promptTemplates.prompt: Use tree-sitter to parse this Python code and extract all function definitions with their names and parameters.

promptTemplates.title: Build AST visitor
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an AST visitor class that walks the parse tree and collects all class definitions.

promptTemplates.title: Write query pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a tree-sitter query to find all decorated functions with @pytest.mark decorators.

promptTemplates.title: Handle errors
promptTemplates.prompt: Parse this code with error handling. Return both the tree and any parse errors encountered.

bestPractices: Always implement error handling for parse errors and missing nodes
Use tree-sitter queries instead of manual tree traversal when possible
Test query patterns on real code samples before production use
antiPatterns: Assuming all source files parse successfully without error handling
Using manual child iteration instead of optimized query patterns
Ignoring performance implications when parsing large codebases

faq.question: What languages does tree-sitter support?
faq.answer: Tree-sitter supports 40+ languages including Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, Rust, Ruby, C, C++, and many more.

faq.question: How do I install language parsers?
faq.answer: Install parsers via npm (tree-sitter-javascript), pip (tree-sitter-python), or compile from the official tree-sitter-lang repos.

faq.question: Can I use this with any framework?
faq.answer: Yes. Patterns are framework-agnostic. Use with Code-Index-MCP, treesitter-chunker, or custom implementations.

faq.question: Is parsed code executed?
faq.answer: No. Tree-sitter only parses source text into an AST. It never executes or evaluates the parsed code.

faq.question: Why are my queries returning no matches?
faq.answer: Check node type names match the language grammar. Use (editor.tree-sitter-query-test) to verify queries against sample code.

faq.question: How is this different from regex?
faq.answer: Tree-sitter understands code structure via the AST. Queries can match nested patterns that regex cannot capture.
